speaker: interviewer
text: --of the interview with Lillian Bielsky-Bell, December 1st, 1996. So what happened when the SS came in? How did things change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Changed briefly, and very suddenly from good to bad. They-- first of all, they placed us in special streets, which they, for each, you know, they made up their own minds. Like, our street went, across the barracks-- from the army barracks that went from hand to hand, you know, from the Poles to the Russians, from the Russians to the Germans. They give-- there was a lot of the people that worked there. They had so-called scheins. Scheins, it means [? indications ?] of permits that they are allowed to work there. And they were privileged. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I worked in Judenrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the Judenrat established?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat was established the minute they put us in-- into-- it called [NON-ENGLISH]. It called sections. But it wasn't a closed ghetto. Then they put on already the yellow signs on us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe that yellow sign?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like a star.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it that way to begin with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a yellow star, like a star. And you-- if you were caught without it, and they knew that you were Jewish it was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you have to wear this star?

speaker: interviewee
text: On top of every clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you show me where you wore it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right here.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the front and the back, or just on the front?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just on the front. And everybody had to be occupied. That's when the Juden-- Judenrat was formed. And that's when the-- what did they-- the Germans gave orders to the Judenrat what to do, how to occupy the people, where to send them, what they need, if they need help for them, if they need to feed their dogs, if they need to-- By the way, I must stop here, because right after the Germans came in-- oh, this was already-- I'm sorry. This was already when the ghetto was, after the first slaughter. And I worked like a messenger in Judenrat. So in other words, there was no phones. There was no communication. So if the Judenrat had to tell somebody from my neighbors, or somebody at the street, you have to be, at 5 o'clock, at work, and you go to work here and here, come to the Judenrat, get your instructions, and go to work. But I had to go and give the message, because there was no other way of communicating with those people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that job have a name? Did you have a title?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a messenger.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you know anybody on the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you get that job?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had knew somebody that worked there, and he was in-- the head of the Judenrat, and he gave me that job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [? Laufki ?] Glauberman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that considered a good job to have?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CHUCKLES] I did not have the privileges what my-- my stepbrother had. He had much more privileges. And after a while you'll see that he had the privileges. Because of-- as of May 8, when we were slaughters, and it was the first [NON-ENGLISH], on account of him with his schein, with his identification, we remained alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What were some of the messages that you had to take from-- from the Judenrat, back and forth? Not back and forth, just from the Judenrat to the people.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. What were some of the messages?

speaker: interviewer
text: That you'll have to come to work at 5 o'clock. Be here and here. That you have to be at 10 o'clock, here and here. Come to the Judenrat. They have-- they have to send you. You could not say no, because the Judenrat was ordered by-- by the SS, by the Nazis, by the Gebietskommissar. Do you know who was the Gebietskommissar? Do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The worst that I hated, that it was Werner. And the other one I knew, too, but I forgot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In fact, he was-- he had a trial in Germany. I know the leader-- the [? born ?] leader that were there. They-- they went for the trial to be witnesses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there someone named Hanwig?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. [INAUDIBLE] I know Werner, because his dogs I remember best. That was his--

speaker: interviewer
text: What about his dogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: What about his dogs? After the first slaughter, May the 8th, 1942, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they then went, and they placed us already in a closed ghetto-- in a wired ghetto, in a closed ghetto, with five, six families in a house. And the next morning we had to go to work. So what did we do? We had to clean up the-- the mess from the burning buildings, the bricks, to put it on such a, you know-- I forgot whatever you call this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wagons?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not wagons. You had to carry it. You couldn't wheel it. And in Polish, it's [NON-ENGLISH], but in English, I don't know how to say it. And you used to put on plenty of the-- from the house, I just said it-- the-- the bricks. And you had to take this away from this place, and place it in a place where a truck came-- a German truck came. And they used to take it away and throw it away. You had to clean out the spaces where the buildings were completely clean, like never nothing happened. And Sunday, here was this Werner, used to-- Winnisch, Winnisch was one of-- of the commanders in the Gebietskommissar. Windisch, Windisch. And Sunday after breakfast, after all the luxury that he had-- the-- the bacon with-- with all the goodies-- he used to let the dogs down, feed them, too, with a very good breakfast-- much better than the people that had in the ghettos-- and then let them jump on the people. Let them free, and jump on the people that worked across the Gebietskommissar by cleaning up the-- there was plenty of times that they tear a person in-- in pieces. It's a funny story that here is my partner that used to schlep with me the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bricks?

speaker: interviewee
text: --bricks. And I could never remember. And then she one time we met here in a store. She had a store in [? Fins. ?] And she says to me, now I must tell you this story. I said, what story will you tell me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when we were partners in schlepping the bricks? You were always the first one in front, and I was in the back. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I don't know, I never thought about it. She said, I'll tell you why. Because when he used to let the-- the dogs down on Sunday. So whenever he felt like to make a show, he-- you should be the first one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was hysterical. She was hysterical. She told-- told this as a joke. Believe me, I know that she-- at that time even didn't cross her mind that it could something happen like this. But now, when we came here, and when we met, there was one time in her store she says to me, you never thought about it, why you were always in the front and I in the back? You told me earlier today that when the Germans came in, your father's wife wanted to sell you to the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a [INAUDIBLE]. This is a different story. This is already after the slaughter when we survived on design of my-- we were at that time, her-- my stepmother's two sisters and mother lived with us when we were placed, I told you, across the army barracks, and when slowly, slowly they started to form a ghetto. But before they formed the ghetto they made a [NON-ENGLISH], a slaughter, the first time. It was May 8th, 1942. By that time Tavia came already a few times to-- to Lida as a Pollack, trying to spot us, and to tell us to be very careful, and to get out of there, to escape from Lida. But my father was a genius. He never wanted what will be [NON-ENGLISH], he used to say, [NON-ENGLISH]. I am not doing anything different than anybody else. I'm not privileged. If we have to go, we'll go all together. And he didn't want even to listen. But quite a few times he came on-- on a Russian-- on the Polish papers. Instead Belski, Belaruski, Belaruski with big-- with a big mustache, with long hair. But with all that, at certain times somebody recognized him, and he had to escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when he left Lida?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh he left Lida when Lida burned. And he never returned to it. Never returned. He went to his father. Then he went from night to night with her, with her, with Sonia at one night. But this peasant-- she was sitting by a peasant. And she was sewing there, she was during alteration. But he was going from one place to the other, and where he slept he didn't eat, and where he ate he didn't sleep. But he came quite a few times to the ghetto to warn us. And then they, in May, when it was the slaughter, we survived just under sign from my stepfather, stepbrother, because he was privileged working for the Germans in the-- for the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what happened May 8th, 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'll tell you what. May 8th, 19-- 1942, they-- it was already rumors all over that some day will happen. Something is going on. They smelled in the air that something is going on. But you know, when it doesn't happen, you don't believe. And all of a sudden, in the morning, we got up. And my father says to me, I dreamt a dream. And I want you to know that today, they way I look out the window, and they're going from house to house, I think today is the day. So whatever it will happen to me, I want you to survive. You are young, you have to survive. With me, 48, 49-- he wasn't 49. He was 47 at that time. Whatever it will happen to me, I'm old. But you have to survive, and try to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I started to scream at him. Aye, Papa! With this, and Papa that, stop talking. A-- a dream. Since when are you believing in dreams?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, get dressed, because I see they're very close to our house already, the Germans. And I was still in a nightgown. And unbelievable, May 8th should snow. And it was like flakes. Not a heavy snow, but flakes. Sure enough, they caught me in my nightgown and barefoot, and that's how they chased me out from my house. My father went down to the basement to put away some gold and money in case somebody will survive. So he took out a brick. But somebody followed him and took out the money, and took with him and went-- and went. And we came to the place. And there were left and right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They send us to the left. They-- we weren't all five of us, me and my father, my stepmother and my stepbrother, his two aunts, and there, his grandmother. [COUGHS] No, not twins. One was at the peasants doing the alterations. Just the other sister. [COUGHS] The other sister, and the mother. And they knocked with their hand like this, send us to the left, and them to the right. But at that time, nobody knew which side is the right one and which one is the wrong one. [COUGHING] My God. [COUGHING] It's everything on the table. [COUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you have to-- when they chased you out of your house, where did you have to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: [BLOWS NOSE] They send us to a place, Piastowska, where they make the [? Firschel ?] ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that far from where you were living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pretty far. [COUGHING] Oh my God. [BLOWS NOSE] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you went there with your father, and your stepmother--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my stepbrother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And those relatives.

speaker: interviewee
text: No relatives. They were sent to the right. While they weren't sent to our side, and they didn't come to ghetto, we knew they-- that the right side was dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a selection before the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I told you, the German put his hand like this. These two, he sent to the right. Us he sent to the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who that was, who did the selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's all what I have to know. You know how many it was there? How many machine guns? And on the way to the ghetto, the-- the shooting was just incredible. You could get deaf listening to the shooting. [BLOWS NOSE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the shooting on the place where they did the selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they chased you away immediately, as soon as you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody took us one, and the command of the SS took us to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you heard the sounds of the shooting from that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Going there we heard it-- that was Piastowska, there was terrible shooting. And on top of everything, they told us that tomorrow at 6:00 in the morning we have to be ready to go to work. Somebody will be there to take us to work. And they'll assign us to all kind of jobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were there in your nightgown and barefoot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went into a house-- you-- you-- they didn't select for you the house. Wherever you went get in, because the people were taken from there. [BLOWS NOSE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that area, that Piastowska area, that had-- Jews had lived there just before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Before. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was also part of those designated streets.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When did they put up the barbed wire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very soon. Maybe a day or two after everybody, they settled down, and we settled down a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, the next morning, when you had to go to work, were you still in your nightgown?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Then I found clothing. I found clothing, you know. I had-- I first of all, I had what to wear it with me. But I couldn't, because they chased my-- and there is no monkey business with them. If they tell you to go, they go. If you don't go, if you move-- make one move-- they shoot you. They came to their-- their house, and there in the house were different clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. At what point did-- did you stepbrother's schein help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: On his schein we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they look at your papers at the selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: At his paper, yeah. And he said, it's my family. And this wasn't all of it. So when we came there, next day-- this was almost in the middle of the-- very late in the evening. So we were ordered to the next morning to be ready for work. And I had my watch on. And I asked a Polish guy with a German that was on duty what time it is-- not to be late, not to-- to-- you know, not to get any trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I looked at my watch. And I put my watch correct accordingly. And I went into the house. I went into the house. I went in. You know how many people it was in the house, you know, that you knew, that you didn't know. And I find a spot to go to sleep. And so it's a knock on the door. Who is it? Who is it? Everybody was shivering. Police and a German. Where is the girl? And my father didn't know nothing, that I ask about what time it is, nothing. Where is the girl that asked what time it is? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, what they used to do, if somebody from the parents were alive and didn't-- and didn't went to the right side, they tried to protect their kid. So the youngsters, they pushed away in a different corner. They shouldn't see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, who are you looking for? And he describes, so my father knew, more or less, that it's me. So he says, what do you want? What did she do? So he says, no, she didn't do nothing, but she asked what time it is. I want the girl right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: What are you going to do to the girl, my father asked. He says, that's my business. That's not your business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was having this conversation with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German and the Pole. So my father said, if you intend to do something bad to her, or to kill her, or to what, here I'm ready. Take me. No, they said. We want her. It didn't help. I had to come out. My father had to call me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they didn't do nothing to me. They just made it a game. Scared the guts out of everybody. Took away my watch that I had, and went out. But the game, the nerves, the-- the fear which way you'll die. You weren't secured for one second that you'll-- that you'll live. That nothing will happen to you. Before that selection, and-- and before you were put into that ghetto, did they take possessions away from Jews? Did you have to give in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, gold.

speaker: interviewer
text: --jewelry or go--

speaker: interviewee
text: Every Sunday, every other Sunday, they used to make shows on the-- in the marketplace where it was-- when-- where the peasants used to bring in and sell their stuff. They used to bring 50 the most-- the intellectuals, the-- the most intelligent people on the place, an empty place, and play around. Some of them they used to shoot. Some of them they let it go, but every time they made it like a game. It was just a show to play with somebody's life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: One time-- they used to take everybody, not once, and gather them in the marketplace, or in another place, and then they took this one, this one, this one, and the rest go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you were forced to go to watch that.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're not forced. I didn't know if I won't be the next one. When you went there, nobody knew what will happen to you. Whoever they liked, whoever they didn't like, whatever came to their mind, that's what they did. We weren't human beings. We were animals in their eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the Judenrat singled out for special treatment on those occasions?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Was threatened as good as everybody-- as every Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the same Judenrat at the beginning as it was after May 8th, 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. After the May-- after May 8th it was a different Judenrat, and a different space, and different orders. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was the Judenrat different?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? Because they had to put our own police people. In other words, they had to help. They had to watch the-- the in and out of the ghetto, you know. Nobody should escape. Nobody should, in the middle of the night, go. They looked like this. But they had to follow the Germans' orders to do that. In other words, the Judenrat got bigger. You know. When we all gathered in one place, that we weren't separated to here, one is here, one is there. But when after May 8th was one ghetto. So everything was-- had to be bigger. All right. And that day there was killed 5,500 people, May 8th.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So how was your life different in this smaller ghetto afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: My live in the smaller ghetto was different, because Tavia used to send a lot of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people that worked with him, that he had all the confidence in them, and to warn us, and to talk to my father. My father never-- he couldn't come to the ghetto. To the closed ghetto he didn't come already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tavia. But he used to send people with letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did these letters say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Get out. Don't wait for another. I told you that this would happen. Lucky you survived, but get out, because another one will come. In the woods, I don't guarantee nothing, but at least there is a lot of trees, and you can hide under a tree, and you're free. Here you are locked up. My father didn't want to hear. And one time, I was assigned to the gardens to take care of the vegetables and the fruit. Not fruit, but mostly the vegetables-- tremendous gardens that supplied a lot of vegetables, potatoes, onions. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they used to take us from the ghetto in the morning by police and a German. And in a certain hour-- in a certain hour we had to come back after sun-- sunset. But you couldn't go alone. After 6 o'clock nobody, could be seen alone in the street, a Jew. Because this was a curfew. And whoever they met-- if they met somebody, that's no-- no playing around. It was right away they shot it, on the spot. So I was assigned to the gardens. And my stepmother was assigned there, too. There was a Pollack that he watched the gardens. And he was in charge of it. And I-- he-- she had always an idea that she'll hide in case of something. Any rumor she heard. She didn't care if my father was home. But she was already-- she never went back from work. She went upstairs into the-- to the-- the person that watched us. He lived there. And she went to him. It was like a barn. And there he used to hide, [? them ?] for money, for money. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: One day he comes up to me. I didn't know nothing. He comes up to me. He says, you know, today's a lot of work, I have to keep you a little later. I said, how will I get to the ghetto? If I'll go all by myself I'll be killed. He said, we'll-- we'll think about it later. Listen, I am a nothing. I cannot fight a fly, even. I'm a Jew. And he was a Pole?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Pole.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember his name. And he says to me, after 6:00, when everybody will finish, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you'll come to me, and then I'll assign you to the work that I want you to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to stop you, even though it's in the middle, because the tape is coming to an end. So we'll start the next tape just at this spot.
